topic: Usted (formal you) and the attribute pronoun lo

tips.title: Usted borrows the he/she verb form
tips.body: Even though usted means 'you,' it conjugates just like él or ella: usted es, not usted eres. That quirk exists because usted grew out of an old title, 'vuestra merced' (your grace), which was grammatically third person — a habit that stuck around for centuries.

tips.title: Lo can stand in for a whole idea, not just a noun
tips.body: You already used lo as 'him/it.' After ser (or estar), lo can also replace an adjective or a whole idea from the question: 'Are you tired? Lo estoy.' or 'Is she the teacher? Lo es.' It doesn't change for gender or number — lo always stays lo.

tips.title: When to reach for usted
tips.body: Use usted with strangers, elders, doctors, teachers, or anyone in a professional setting where you want to show respect or keep some distance. If you're unsure which 'you' to use, usted is almost always the safer default.

tips.title: Don't let lo agree with anything
tips.body: A common mistake is trying to make lo match the gender or number of what it replaces, like turning it into 'los' for a plural subject. Resist that urge: 'Son ricos? Lo son.' — lo never changes shape, no matter what it's standing in for.

bonusCards.english: Are you the manager, sir? Yes, I am.
Is the coffee good? I believe so.
Are they the owners? Yes, they are.
Is she the doctor? I believe so.
